annotations.label: Binomial distribution

anchor.snippet: the binomial distribution with parameters n and p is the discrete probability distribution of the number of successes

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the binomial distribution both as a measure on ℕ (`ProbabilityTheory.binomial`) and as a PMF (`PMF.binomial`).

annotations.label: Bernoulli trial

anchor.snippet: A single success/failure experiment is also called a Bernoulli trial or Bernoulli experiment

annotations.note: `PMF.bernoulli p h` is the success/failure distribution on `Bool` modelling a single Bernoulli trial.

annotations.label: Bernoulli process

anchor.snippet: a sequence of outcomes is called a Bernoulli process

annotations.note: `setBernoulli` is a product of Bernoulli distributions over an index set, but a Bernoulli process as an indexed sequence of i.i.d. Bernoulli variables is not defined under that name.

annotations.label: n=1 is Bernoulli distribution

anchor.snippet: when n = 1 , the binomial distribution is a Bernoulli distribution

annotations.note: `PMF.binomial_one_eq_bernoulli` proves `binomial p h 1` equals the Bernoulli distribution mapped to `{0,1}`.

annotations.label: Sampling without replacement gives hypergeometric

anchor.snippet: If the sampling is carried out without replacement, the draws are not independent and so the resulting distribution is a hypergeometric distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no hypergeometric distribution (only the unrelated hypergeometric special function).

annotations.label: Probability mass function

anchor.snippet: The probability of getting exactly k successes in n independent Bernoulli trials (with the same rate p ) is given by the probability mass function

annotations.note: `PMF.binomial_apply` gives `p^k (1-p)^(n-k) C(n,k)`, exactly the binomial PMF.

anchors.snippet: The probability of getting exactly k successes in n independent Bernoulli trials (with the same rate p ) is given by the probability mass function

anchor.snippet: the probability can be calculated by its complement as

annotations.note: The p↔(1-p), k↔(n-k) symmetry of the binomial PMF is not formalized as a lemma.

anchor.snippet: There is always an integer M that satisfies

annotations.note: No result about the mode-determining integer M of the binomial PMF exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monotonicity and mode of PMF

anchor.snippet: is monotone increasing for k < M and monotone decreasing for k > M

annotations.note: Monotonicity of the binomial PMF around its mode is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: It can also be represented in terms of the regularized incomplete beta function

annotations.label: Moment-generating function

anchor.snippet: The moment-generating function is

annotations.note: The general MGF concept is defined, but the binomial MGF formula `(1-p+pe^t)^n` is not computed.

annotations.label: Mode formula

anchor.snippet: Usually the mode of a binomial B ( n , p ) distribution is equal to

annotations.note: The mode `⌊(n+1)p⌋` of the binomial distribution is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Usually the mode of a binomial B ( n , p ) distribution is equal to

annotations.label: Mean, median, mode coincide when np integer

anchor.snippet: If np is an integer, then the mean, median, and mode coincide and equal np

annotations.note: No median results for the binomial distribution are formalized.

annotations.label: Median interval bound

anchor.snippet: Any median m must lie within the interval

annotations.note: The bound `⌊np⌋ ≤ m ≤ ⌈np⌉` on the binomial median is not formalized.

annotations.label: Median close to mean

anchor.snippet: A median m cannot lie too far away from the mean

annotations.note: The `|m - np| ≤ max(p, 1-p)` median bound is not formalized.

annotations.label: Median unique equals round(np)

anchor.snippet: The median is unique and equal to m = round ( np ) when

annotations.note: No uniqueness/round(np) median result is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Median unique when p rational

anchor.snippet: When p is a rational number (with the exception of p = 1/2 and n odd), the median is unique

annotations.note: Median uniqueness for rational p is not formalized.

annotations.label: Median for p=1/2, n odd

anchor.snippet: and n is odd, any number m in the interval

annotations.note: The non-unique median interval for p=1/2, odd n is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hoeffding tail bound

anchor.snippet: Hoeffding's inequality yields the simple bound

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the general Hoeffding inequality for sums of independent sub-Gaussian variables, which generalizes the binomial bound but is not specialized to it.

anchors.snippet: Hoeffding's inequality yields the simple bound

annotations.label: Chernoff tail bound

anchor.snippet: A sharper bound can be obtained from the Chernoff bound

annotations.note: The generic Chernoff bound via the cumulant/moment-generating function exists but is not applied to the binomial distribution.

anchors.snippet: A sharper bound can be obtained from the Chernoff bound

annotations.label: Anti-concentration lower bound

anchor.snippet: known as anti-concentration bounds

annotations.note: Anti-concentration lower bounds for the binomial tail are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: known as anti-concentration bounds

annotations.label: Constant-denominator bound for p=1/2

anchor.snippet: it is possible to make the denominator constant

annotations.note: This specialized p=1/2 tail bound is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: it is possible to make the denominator constant

annotations.label: Estimator of p by proportion of successes

anchor.snippet: the parameter p can be estimated using the proportion of successes

annotations.note: Statistical point estimation of the binomial parameter is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the parameter p can be estimated using the proportion of successes

annotations.label: Bayes estimator with Beta prior

anchor.snippet: A closed form Bayes estimator for p also exists when using the Beta distribution as a conjugate prior distribution

annotations.note: Bayesian conjugate-prior estimation for the binomial is not formalized (only the Beta PDF exists).

anchors.snippet: A closed form Bayes estimator for p also exists when using the Beta distribution as a conjugate prior distribution

annotations.label: Rule of succession

anchor.snippet: This method is called the rule of succession

annotations.note: Laplace's rule of succession is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: This method is called the rule of succession

anchor.snippet: When relying on Jeffreys prior , the prior is

annotations.note: This estimation rule-of-thumb for rare events is not formalized.

annotations.label: Wald confidence interval

anchor.snippet: A continuity correction of 0.5 / n may be added

annotations.note: Confidence intervals for the binomial proportion are not formalized.

anchor.snippet: Here the estimate of p is modified to

annotations.note: The Agresti–Coull interval is not formalized.

annotations.label: Wilson (score) interval

anchor.snippet: this formula does not use a plus-minus to define the two bounds

annotations.note: The Wilson score interval is not formalized.

annotations.label: Clopper–Pearson most conservative

anchor.snippet: is the most conservative

annotations.note: Comparisons of confidence-interval methods are not formalized.

annotations.label: Wald method most biased

anchor.snippet: is the most biased

annotations.note: Bias comparison of interval methods is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sum of independent binomials

anchor.snippet: then X + Y is again a binomial variable

annotations.note: Closure of the binomial family under sums of independent variables (`B(n,p)+B(m,p)=B(n+m,p)`) is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: then X + Y is again a binomial variable

annotations.label: Binomial as special Poisson binomial

anchor.snippet: The binomial distribution is a special case of the Poisson binomial distribution

annotations.note: The Poisson binomial distribution is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Log-ratio of two binomials

anchor.snippet: Then log( T ) is approximately normally distributed with mean log( p 1 / p 2 )

annotations.note: The asymptotic normality of the log-ratio of two binomial estimators is not formalized.

annotations.label: Conditional binomial

anchor.snippet: then Y is a simple binomial random variable with distribution

annotations.note: The conditional-binomial thinning result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bernoulli as special binomial

anchor.snippet: The Bernoulli distribution is a special case of the binomial distribution, where n = 1

annotations.note: `PMF.binomial_one_eq_bernoulli` directly identifies `binomial p h 1` with the Bernoulli distribution.

annotations.label: Normal approximation to binomial

anchor.snippet: a reasonable approximation to B( n , p ) is given by the normal distribution

annotations.note: The general 1-D central limit theorem underlies the normal approximation but is not specialized to binomial sums.

anchors.snippet: a reasonable approximation to B( n , p ) is given by the normal distribution

annotations.label: Skewness rule of thumb

anchor.snippet: the normal approximation is adequate if the absolute value of the skewness is strictly less than 0.3

annotations.note: This heuristic accuracy criterion is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the normal approximation is adequate if the absolute value of the skewness is strictly less than 0.3

annotations.label: 3-standard-deviation rule

anchor.snippet: the normal approximation is appropriate only if everything within 3 standard deviations of its mean is within the range of possible values

annotations.note: This rule-of-thumb is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the normal approximation is appropriate only if everything within 3 standard deviations of its mean is within the range of possible values

anchor.snippet: both values np and n (1 − p ) must be greater than

annotations.note: This heuristic threshold is not formalized.

annotations.label: Continuity correction example

anchor.snippet: The following is an example of applying a continuity correction

annotations.note: Continuity-correction worked examples are not formalized.

annotations.label: de Moivre–Laplace theorem

anchor.snippet: This approximation, known as de Moivre–Laplace theorem

annotations.note: The general CLT subsumes de Moivre–Laplace, but the binomial-specific de Moivre–Laplace theorem is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Poisson approximation

anchor.snippet: The binomial distribution converges towards the Poisson distribution as the number of trials goes to infinity

annotations.note: This theorem proves the binomial probabilities converge to the Poisson PMF when `n·p → r`.

annotations.label: Poisson limit theorem

anchor.snippet: As n approaches ∞ and p approaches 0 with the product np held fixed

annotations.note: `tendsto_choose_mul_pow_of_tendsto_mul_atTop` is exactly the Poisson limit theorem for fixed `np`.

annotations.label: de Moivre–Laplace (limiting form)

anchor.snippet: As n approaches ∞ while p remains fixed

annotations.note: The Gaussian limit for fixed p is a special case of Mathlib's general CLT but is not stated for the binomial.

anchors.snippet: As n approaches ∞ while p remains fixed

annotations.label: Beta–binomial relation

anchor.snippet: the beta distribution and the binomial distribution are related by

annotations.note: The Beta PDF exists (`ProbabilityTheory.betaPDFReal`) but its CDF identity relating Beta and binomial tails is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the beta distribution and the binomial distribution are related by

annotations.label: Posterior is beta under uniform prior

anchor.snippet: the posterior distribution for the probability of success p given n independent events with k observed successes is a beta distribution

annotations.note: Bayesian posterior conjugacy of Beta with the binomial likelihood is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the posterior distribution for the probability of success p given n independent events with k observed successes is a beta distribution

annotations.label: Inversion algorithm for random generation

anchor.snippet: One way to generate random variates samples from a binomial distribution is to use an inversion algorithm

annotations.note: Random-variate generation algorithms are out of scope for Mathlib and not present.
